text: The surgeon begins the gastric bypass procedure using the da Vinci XI robotic system, placing ports on the patient's abdomen to facilitate robotic instrument access.
The surgeon positions the robotic ports in a straight line directed toward the upper abdomen, preparing for the gastric bypass procedure.
The surgeon places the assistant port just to the right of the umbilicus using a non-bladed optical entry trocar, preparing for the gastric bypass procedure.
The surgeon uses the camera through the assistant port to place the eight and a half millimeter robotic operating ports, including a twelve millimeter robotic stapler port, in the upper abdomen.
I like it in my right hand. You could also put it in your left hand on the other side.
The surgeon begins the gastric bypass operation along the lesser curvature of the stomach, creating a window into the lesser sac. The ender wrist stapler is used to perform this step.
The surgeon uses the wristed function of the stapler to create the gastric pouch, demonstrating precise control and maneuverability in the upper abdomen.
The surgeon adjusts the angle of the stapler to create the gastric pouch, utilizing various approaches to ensure precise formation along the lesser curvature.

The surgeon creates the gastric pouch over a visible forty French dilator, which is used to calibrate the pouch size. Seam guards are not used during the initial firings where the anastomosis will be formed.

The surgeon applies seam guards along part of the gastric pouch, demonstrating how they can shift and become cumbersome, requiring reapplication. The robotic stapler's intelligent sensing feature is highlighted during this process.
The surgeon completes the staple line at the top of the gastric pouch without using a seam guard, ensuring secure staple formation.
The surgeon locates the transverse colon and uses a vessel sealer to divide the omentum, clearing the way for an anti-colic gastric bypass.

The surgeon uses an instrument to divide the fatty, thick tissue of the omentum or mesentery. The intestinal loop is positioned approximately fifty centimeters distal to the ligament of Treitz, and the surgeon begins creating a side-to-side anastomosis.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to create a gastrotomy in the gastric pouch, carefully gauging the white line to ensure the opening is made while also sealing the tissue. The surgeon pulls the tissue to open the gastrotomy, noting that scissors could alternatively be used for this step.
The surgeon uses an endoverous stapler to create a side-to-side anastomosis between the small pouch and the bowel, carefully positioning the stapler despite the challenging anatomy.

The surgeon divides the biliary pancreatic limb to create the gastrojejunostomy for the Roux-en-Y gastric bypass. The third arm of the surgical robot is actively used to hold and position tissues during the procedure.
The surgeon places an NG tube across the anastomosis to facilitate the closure of the enterotomy left by the stapler. An absorbable 2-0 VLOC suture is used to complete the gastrojejunostomy.

The surgeon uses a V-lock suture to create a tight suture line without gaps, beginning with a loop that eliminates the need for knot tying. A running full-thickness closure is performed to secure the gastrojejunostomy.
The surgeon uses a running suture to close a small defect, then reverses direction to apply serosal stitches, burying the initial suture line and creating a second line in an open fashion.
The surgeon uses a single suture to create a full thickness suture line, running it upwards, followed by serosal stitches running back down, efficiently completing the closure.

The surgeon uses the third arm of the robotic system to hold the bowel steady while creating enterotomies for the jejuno-jejunostomy using a vessel sealer. The vessel sealer is positioned carefully to ensure precise incisions.

The surgeon uses the vessel sealer to cut and seal the tissue, stretching it open again as needed. Scissors or other instruments could be used, but the vessel sealer is preferred to avoid opening additional instruments.

The surgeon uses a white load wristed stapler to create a side-to-side jejuno-jejunostomy, positioning the stapler precisely for the anastomosis. The stapler is forty-five centimeters in length, contrasting with previous open procedures that used a seventy-five millimeter stapler and endoscopic staplers of sixty millimeters.
The surgeon uses a stapler to extend the anastomosis length from 45 millimeters to approximately 90 millimeters, ensuring a longer connection to prevent narrowing.
The surgeon closes the enterotomy in the small bowel, placing a silk crotch stitch at the end of the staple line to secure the closure.

The surgeon closes the enterotomy on the jejuno-jejunostomy using an absorbable V-lock suture, performing a running full-thickness closure along the small enterotomy. Upon reaching the top, the surgeon reverses direction to apply a second layer of serosal stitches, effectively burying the first layer.

The surgeon creates a tight, secure suture line along the mesentery, using a single suture to close the mesentery defect. The third arm of the surgical instrument holds the mesentery in position, allowing efficient stitching across the defect.

The surgeon completes a two-layer closure on the enterotomy, followed by a mesentery closure using a V-lock suture. The tail of the V-lock is used to finalize the closure, with options for additional stitches to relieve tension. The operation is facilitated by the use of an ender wrist stapler.
If you'd like any more information on this or other robotic procedures, check out my website at clarkgerhart.com.
